content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

June 7, 2019

John Mullen
City Attorney
300 North Coast Highway
Suite 300
Oceanside, CA 92054

Re:     Your Request for Advice
        Our File No. A-19-110

Dear Mr. Mullen:

     This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Oceanside City Council Member Christopher Rodriguez regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

     Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090.

     Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice.

QUESTION

     May Council Member Rodriguez participate in decisions regarding the draft short-term vacation rental ordinance proposed for consideration by the Oceanside City Council, despite the fact that he holds a business position with a firm that currently manages homes registered with the City as short-term vacation rental properties? If not, does the public generally exception in Regulation 18703 apply?

CONCLUSION

     No. The Act prohibits Council Member Rodriguez from participation in decisions regarding the draft short-term vacation rental ordinance. There are no facts to suggest that the public generally exception applies.

___________________________
¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-19-110
Page No. 2

FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER

Oceanside does not currently have any specific ordinance regulating short term vacation
rentals. The City has approximately 64,000 dwelling units according to statistics maintained by the
California Department of Finance. Oceanside's practice has been to permit all housing units to be
used as short-term vacation rentals provided the owner and/or agent registers and pays transient
occupancy tax in accordance with the requirements of Chapter 34 of the Oceanside City Code. City
records document at least 950 vacation rental properties are registered to pay transient occupancy
tax.

In 2018, the Oceanside Planning Commission created an ad hoc committee to study the
issue of short-term rentals and make recommendations to the full Planning Commission. As a result
of this process, City staff have drafted a proposed ordinance for council consideration. The draft
ordinance, if adopted, will require the owner of a dwelling unit used as a vacation rental for less
than thirty consecutive days to obtain a permit to be issued by the City Manager or her designee.
Certain rental properties are exempt from the proposed permit requirement, including: 1) "hosted"
units; and 2) properties managed by a homeowner association with at least 50 units, on site
management and 24-hour security. The draft ordinance bans short-term vacation rentals in specified
mobile home parks, but otherwise permits them in all residential zoning districts. A hosted unit is
defined as one in which the owner resides in the property and offers a portion of the unit for use as
short-term vacation rental. The draft ordinance requires the payment of a fee established by
resolution of the City Council. While the fee has not yet been established, staff anticipates
recommending an annual fee of $250 in order to administer the ordinance. The ordinance includes
detailed operational requirements designed to mitigate the adverse impacts that short-term rental
properties create in neighborhoods, including regulations to reduce noise, ensure sufficient parking,
eliminated trash, and establish maximum occupancy.

The draft ordinance was recommended for approval by the Oceanside Planning Commission
with the addition that new "non-hosted" short-term vacation rentals be prohibited in all residential
zoning districts, except the Residential Tourist ("RT") district and downtown Oceanside. The
Commission's recommendation would allow all existing registered short-term rentals to continue in
all residential zoning districts (including single family residential "RS") and new "hosted" vacation
rentals would be allowed in all residential zoning districts. Staff will present the Commission's
recommendations to the City Council on May 29, 2019, but does not intend to endorse it. The staff
proposed draft ordinance, if adopted, will allow short-term vacation rentals in all residential zoning
districts subject to the specified permitting, fee, and operational requirements

Council Member Rodriguez is the Chief Executive Officer of Maximum Mortgage and Real
Estate, Inc. ("Maximum"), a California corporation. Maximum provides mortgage brokerage, real
estate and property management services. Maximum currently manages three Oceanside single
family homes registered with the City as short-term vacation rental properties. However, Maximum
does not have an ownership interest in any of these three properties. These three properties are all
located in the single family (RS) zone. Maximum is paid twenty percent of gross revenues for its
property management services and generates approximately $2,200 a month for its management of
these three short-term vacation rentals.

File No. A-19-110
Page No. 3

The draft ordinance recommended by staff, if adopted, will require the owner of these three
properties to obtain a short-term rental permit at an anticipated annual cost of $250. This
same requirement is proposed to be applied to all other 950 registered short-term rental properties,
and any future short-term rental permit allowed under the ordinance. The permit would have to be
renewed annually, and the owner of the dwelling unit would be subject to the operational
requirements of the ordinance. If the Planning Commission's proposed amendment is adopted, all
three properties may continue to be used as short-term vacation rentals provided the owner obtains
the necessary annual permit, pays the required fee, and complies with the operational requirements.
However, if the Planning Commission recommendation is adopted, no new short-term rentals will
be allowed in single family zones unless the owner resides in the unit while it is rented as a short-
term rental.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits public officials from participating in governmental decisions in
which they have a financial interest. Section 87103 provides that a public official has a financial
interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial
effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or her
immediate family, or on any of the interests specified in Section 87103. The financial interests
implicated by our facts are:

• A business entity interest, where an official has a direct or indirect investment of
$2,000 or more in a business entity (Section 87103(a)); or in which the official is
a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of
management (Section 87103(d)).

• An interest in a source of income, aggregating five hundred dollars ($500) or
more in value provided or promised to, received by, the public official within 12
months prior to the time when the decision is made. (Section 87103(c).) This
includes any community property interest in the income of a spouse and a pro rata
share of the income of any business entity or trust in which the official (or his or
her spouse) owns directly, indirectly, or beneficially, a 10-percent or greater
interest. (Section 82030(a).)

• An interest in the official's personal finances, including those of the official's
immediate family. This is known as "the personal financial effects" rule. (Section
87103.)

It appears that Council Member Rodriguez has a business entity interest in Maximum and a
source of income interest in the business.

File No. A-19-110
Page No. 4

Business Entity and Source of Income Interest

Regulation 18702.1 provides the materiality standard for interests in business entities, including business entities that are sources of income to the official.² For a business entity not explicitly involved in a decision, Regulation 18702.1(b) establishes that a foreseeable effect of a decision is material if "a prudent person with sufficient information" would find it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision's financial effect "would contribute to a change in the value of a privately-held business entity." Examples of applicable decisions include those that:

(1) Authorize, prohibit, regulate or otherwise establish conditions for an activity in which the business entity is engaged;

(2) Increase or decrease the amount of competition in the field in which the business entity is engaged;

(3) Increase or decrease the need for the products or services that the business entity supplies;

(4) Make improvements in the surrounding neighborhood such as redevelopment projects, traffic/road improvements, or parking changes that may affect, either temporarily or permanently, the amount of business the business entity receives;

(5) Decide the location of a major development, entertainment facility, or other project that would increase or decrease the amount of business the entity draws from the location of the project; or

(6) Increase or decrease the tax burden, debt, or financial or legal liability of the business entity.

(Regulation 18702.1(b).)

Maximum is paid twenty percent of gross revenues for its property management services and generates approximately $2,200 a month for its management of three short-term vacation rentals. Under the terms of the draft ordinance as recommended by the Planning Commission, no new short-term rentals will be allowed in single family zones unless the owner resides in the unit while it is rented as a short-term rental. A prudent person would likely find that these changes will impact the value of Maximum because the ordinance would decrease the amount of competition in the field in which it is engaged, and decrease the need for the services that Maximum supplies, by limiting new short-term rentals in single family zones. Therefore, it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will materially affect the Council Member's interest in Maximum.

Because of the financial effect on his source of income, Council Member Rodriguez has a conflict of interest that prohibits him from making, participating in making, or using his position to

_____________
² Where the source of income in a business entity, Regulation 18702.3 applies the standards for materiality in Regulation 18702.1.

File No. A-19-110
Page No. 5

influence decisions regarding the draft short-term vacation rental ordinance. There are no facts to
suggest that the public generally exception under Regulation 18703 would apply.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Dave Bainbridge
General Counsel

By: Zachary W. Norton
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

ZWN:aja

sections.question:

fact that he holds a business positon with a firm that currently manages homes registered with the 
City as short-term vacation rental properties? If not, does the public generally exception in 
Regulation 18703 apply?
sections.conclusion:
No. The Act prohibits Council Member Rodriquez from participation in decisions regarding 
the draft short-term vacation rental ordinance. There are no facts to suggest that the public generally 
exception applies.

effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or her

$2,000 or more in a business entity (Section 87103(a)); or in which the official is 
a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of 
management (Section 87103(d)).

An interest in a source of income, aggregating five hundred dollars ($500) or 
more in value provided or promised to, received by, the public official within 12 
months prior to the time when the decision is made. (Section 87103(c).) This 
includes any community property interest in the income of a spouse and a pro rata 
share of the income of any business entity or trust in which the official ( or his or 
her spouse) owns directly, indirectly, or beneficially, a IO-percent or greater 
interest. (Section 82030(a).)

vacation rental ordinance proposed for consideration by the Oceanside City Council, despite the 
fact that he holds a business positon with a firm that currently manages homes registered with the 
City as short-term vacation rental properties? If not, does the public generally exception in 
Regulation 18703 apply?

CONCLUSION: No. The Act prohibits Council Member Rodriquez from participation in decisions regarding 
the draft short-term vacation rental ordinance. There are no facts to suggest that the public generally 
exception applies.
